question: Finding Leads OOP with Strong Hands when your Opponent Lacks Bluffs
answer:
Consider Leading the River When

  • Villain has a non-polarized range that is SD heavy and significantly lacks air combos AND
  • Your hand is worth more than a check AND
  • Your range can support betting

SRP BvB: 2c3c4c xc 6d xc 6c CAN LEAD

SRP MPvsBB: 8c6c9s D33 R50 C 3h B70 C 4c LEAD ALL IN

4B BTNvsBB: Jd5h2c B20 C 3d B25 C 5s CANT LEAD

Do NOT use this play when opponent has a polar turn range containing many non-SD bluffs that will shove river when we check.

question: Calling vs Polarization
answer:
Vs Aggressive Polarization

  • FOLD all of your bluffcatchers vs weak/tight regs - only find calls against aggressive regs and players who are capable of bluffing!
  • Against, players who x/R with proper aggression sometimes you should x-back strong hands IP that don't want to see a x/r and barrel.
  • First consider what they are representing - then try to call with hands that block their value

Most players will NOT be balanced when making large river nuts-or-nothing overbets.

question: Expanding Your Bluff Check-Raises (vs players who C-bet or fold too much
answer:
More bluff check-raising a great exploit vs players who c-bet the flop with too weak of a range - even better if they overfold on later streets.

Things to Look For

Juice spots! Are they c-betting too much? Range betting boards they shouldn't?

Hand Qualities

  1. Make many better hands fold
  2. Interacts with the board such that you'll be able to barrel equity-driven on a lot of turns
  3. Has the potential to turn or river a value-bettable hand
  4. Blocks villain's calling range (on the flop or on future streets)
  5. "Difficult" bluffs - the harder it is to find bluff in a spot, the higher-EV your bluffs will be
  6. "Good" spots - you will get called, but you can increase your chances by choosing your spots (and player profiles) well

question: You Don't Need the Nuts to Overbet (You Just Need the Best Hand)
answer:
A common misconception is that you can only overbet the strongest parts of your range for value - but really all you need is enough vs your opponent's range

Some Rules

  1. The more unbalanced the opponent, the thinner we can overbet
  2. The less you want to bluff a certain player, the thinner you want to value bet them
  3. This strategy really crushes fish and "non-believer" (stick!) regs
  4. The fewer value combos your opponent has that beat your hand, the larger and wider you can go for value
  5. The better you are at breaking down your opponent's actual range, the more of these spots you can find correctly
  6. Employing these plays vs other regs can really hurt their confidence and induce them to make more mistakes vs you in the future

question: The Art of Folding
answer:
Some Rules

  1. It does not matter how strong your hand is - your opponent needs to ahve enough bluff combos to make bluffcatchers a call
  2. Player profiling is super important!
  3. Always try to find the bluff combos in each spot yourself and ask how difficult they are to find
  4. Always try to find the value combos as well - if there is a lot of value relative to bluffs, it's almost always a fold
  5. If you see almost no value but also no bluffs, weight the fact that it's harder to find bluffs over the lack of value and lean towards folding
  6. We all make mistakes - use them as lessons to improve
  7. Always consider their value range relative to the properties of your hand, and determine if those properties help or hurt you

Another good way to increase our blue-line is to find high-level folds in spots where most player would call down

question: Exploitative Node-Locked All-Ins
answer:
One of the fastest ways to increase your win-rate is by punishing the weaker regs and fish for their unbalanced ranges in common nodes

Criteria for Making These Plays

If you can cap your opponent's ranges given the fact they tend to take (or not take) certain actions; i.e. they play pure strategy with certain hand classes in spots where a mixed strategy wold be correct in theory

question: Three High-EV Lessons
answer:
1. 1. Take advanrage of face up range chop pot spots
    • Don't chop when they have too many folds
    • Look for nodes where a particular opponent doesn't slowplay enough OOP
  2. Always take your fold equity when you have a huge draw and your opponent has a lot of potential folds
    • Plan A: Think "How can i win without showdown?"
    • Plan B: The draw coming in
  3. Don't be afraid to make heavy exploitative adjustments
    • Pay attention to showdowns; note strong deviations or tendencies

question: Finding a Raise in -EV or 0EV Call Spots
answer:
Some Rules

  1. You need to clearly see many value combos that your opponent will bet/fold
  2. You want to see as many bluffs in their range as possible
  3. Your line should be credible as a value line
  4. Always "inverser the spot" mentally to explicitly check how much credibility/fold equity the line would have on you

question: When to Flop 3-Bet Bluff to Increase Your Hand's EV
answer:
Some Rules

  1. Can be done both in and out of position
  2. You typically want to use a hand class that has low EV in alternative lines
  3. The wider and weaker your opponent's range is, the more these 3-bets punish them
  4. The less made hands or combo draws that your opponent can have to reraise, the better the spot
  5. Often times very small sizes can be used
  6. If your opponent is very weak/tight and isn't bluffing enough then just bet/fold
  7. Your blockers to their continue range will always be your first choice candidates

question: Finding Unconventional Overbet Bluffs
answer:
The Process

  • Break down your opponent's range street by street on the way to a specific node (be realistic!)
  • Note how non-nutted their range is, and judge how most players would defend that range vs large bets
  • Note the gap between that defense and proper defense

question: Playing Dynamic Ranges
answer:
(Tailored to Counter your Opponent's Range)

The ability to change your betting range on any given street in order to maximally exploit an opponent's strategy (i.e., a mental node-lock).

This is the single-most EV-increasing skill in poker (aside from a good mental game).

  • Always start with a good baseline strategy which will perform well on average - have a starting point for all the streets!
  • Make a read and put players into one of three main categories:

1. Weak Reg/Weak Fish

  • Does not protect checking ranges
  • Overfolds
  • Underbluffs

Plan: Attack their weak, vulnerable ranges.

2. Station/Spewer (Reg or Fish)

  • Likes to bluffcatch
  • Probably overcalls
  • Probably overbluffs (These often go hand in hand!)

Plan: Increase value sizing; lower value threshold.

3. Good, respectable regs

  • Bluffs but doesn't spew
  • Protects ranges
  • Not where your main win-rate comes from

Plan: Stick to baseline; play solid.

question: Playing Dynamic Ranges (Part 2)
answer:
(Tailored to Counter your Opponent's Range)

(Part 2 - vs a Good, Aggressive, Sticky Reg)

Good Bluffing Candidates

  1. Can make many better hands fold
  2. Can pick up equity driven draws or hands that can keep barreling
  3. Can make a value-bettable hand by the river

2. Station/Spewer (Reg or Fish)

  • Likes to bluffcatch
  • Probably overcalls
  • Probably overbluffs

Plan: Increase value sizing; lower value threshold

question: The Art of Hero Calling
answer:
Some Rules

  • Hero calling vs too-strong ranges is the single-fastest way to absolutely DESTROY your win-rate.
  • Hero calling vs too-weak ranges absolutely PRINTS in spots where your opponents don't realize how easy it is for them to have too many bluffs.
  • Good spots to hero call are when you can logically deduce the bluff region and value region and see an asymmetry in the bluff region.
  • Look for spots where there is a very clear value range and clear bluff combos with no showdown value.
  • If you have to make unrealistic assumptions about their range, then it's probably not a good spot.
  • Pot odds are of course also important.

question: Three High EV Concepts
answer:
• 1. Shoving vs unbalanced sizings with strong draws
    If your opponent has overbets in his turn game but is not currently using one (especially on a wet, connected board), this could indicate a weak range.
  • 2. Finding your nut bluffs in every spot
    • Bluffs that are blocking his strongest hands
    • Bluffs that have the most equity when called
  • 3. When you have enough equity to stack off, ALWAYS think about how to allow your opponent to put in more $ with the weakest parts of their range

question: Metagame and Game Flow
answer:
• When you represent a very thin value range to the same player multiple times in succession, your fold equity goes down each time.
  • Keep tabs on who has seen you do what!
  • When your opponent tries to hero call you in a specific line and is wrong, your fold equity goes up in the immediate future.
  • You can preemptively widen your value range vs a specific player when the metagame and game history call for it.
  • The more crazy or non-standard the hand is, the more likely your opponents will take note and adjust.

  • Always rank your hands according to equity on each street.
  • Always use ranges and range analysis first before psychology in poker - but don't ignore it either!

question: Facing 4Bet OOP: Splitting X/R Ranges into Small/Big Sizes. How to do it?
answer:
Facing a 4Bet OOP is not a great node. Once we face a 4B we shift from a high-EV 3B spot to one where we just want to lose as little as possible.

Donking

Having a Donking Range Makes Sense When

  • Your cold call range has flopped multiple sets
  • Your opponent is likely check back (AK combos have missed the board)
  • Your hand class has a clear answer vs raise
    • Don't lead lots of equity hands
    • Don't lead complete trash

Splitting X/R Ranges into Small/Big Sizes

  • Even at low SPRs, OOP should have a raising range vs small (likely range) cbets
  • First, define the bottom of your value bet range - then choose your bluffs
    • Avoid hands that suffer vs shove
  • Generally you want to have an all-in size and a small size
    • All-in range should be draws that want to capitalize on their fold equity plus value hands that need protection
  • You can almost always shove an 8-out draw or better

Make sure you play your range and NOT your specific hand vs regs

  • If the flop checks around and the turn is good for your opponent, don't get too excited with a big hand and leave your checking range unprotected
    • Check shove highest equity draws
    • Slowplay your best value hands

Try to identify spots where the pool doesn't understand their hand's equity vs a range with thin value raises

  • The wider your opponent's range, the better
  • The more sizing tell you can pick up, the better
  • The more loosely they call, the better
  • The more they cbet too weak a range, the better
